read-tracked-and-untracked-state.label: Read the Community Project State
read-tracked-and-untracked-state.summary: Inspect the two untracked files, stage them, and review the proposed snapshot.

sketch1.title: Short Status Is a Compact Map

try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.title: Read the Initial Repository State
try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.prompt: Ask Git for the full repository report. Use it to confirm that the branch is `main`, no commits exist yet, and `README.md` plus `schedule.txt` are untracked. Do not change repository state.
try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.hint: Use Git’s full status report as a read-only inspection.

help.concept: `git status` describes the current branch and separates staged, unstaged, and untracked work. Running it does not change any file or repository layer.
help.hint_1: Look for the `No commits yet` message and the two untracked file names.
help.hint_2: Do not stage either file in this step.

try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.starterCode:
# Community Project

Shared community activities and updates.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf -- community-project
mkdir -p -- community-project
git -C community-project init -q -b main
git -C community-project config --local user.name 'ZoeSkoul Learner'
git -C community-project config --local user.email learner@zoeskoul.local
find community-project -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -name .git -exec rm -rf -- {} +
find community-project -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -name .git -exec rm -rf -- {} +
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s '# Community Project

Shared community activities and updates.
' > community-project/README.md
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community events

Saturday cleanup: 10:00 AM
' > community-project/schedule.txt

try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.title: Stage the Two Project Files
try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.prompt: Prepare the first proposed snapshot by staging only `README.md` and `schedule.txt`. Then inspect compact status and confirm that both paths appear in the staging-area column.
try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.hint: Name the two project files explicitly, then read compact status.

help.concept: `git add README.md schedule.txt` copies the current versions of those two files into the staging area. `git status --short` compresses repository state into two columns; an `A` in the left column means the file is staged as an addition.
help.hint_1: There are only two learner files in this module.
help.hint_2: Staging prepares a proposal; it does not create a commit.

try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.starterCode:

Shared community activities and updates.
try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.solutionCode:
git add README.md schedule.txt
git status --short

try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.title: Review the Staged File Summary
try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.prompt:
Both files are staged, but no commit has been created yet. Use the command you learned earlier to inspect the staged summary and confirm that the proposed snapshot contains exactly two files.

Do not create the commit yet.
try_read_tracked_and_untracked_state_ske.hint: Ask Git for a statistics-only view of the staging area.

help.concept: `git diff --staged --stat` summarizes the files and line counts currently in the staging area. `--staged` reads the proposed snapshot, and `--stat` keeps the output compact. The command does not commit anything.
help.hint_2: Leave both files staged for the next lesson.

single-choice-2.title: Understanding git status --short
single-choice-2.prompt: What does the `??` symbol indicate in the output of `git status --short`?
single-choice-2.hint: In short status, two question marks appear beside a file Git has not started tracking.

help.concept: Read full and short status reports to tell untracked files, unstaged edits, staged work, and clean state apart.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

multi-choice-1.title: Read the Two Columns
multi-choice-1.prompt: Which statements about short status are correct? Select all that apply.
multi-choice-1.hint: Judge each option independently; choose only the statements that stay true in this exact repository state.

help.concept: Read full and short status reports to tell untracked files, unstaged edits, staged work, and clean state apart.
help.hint_1: A partly true option is still wrong if it adds an effect Git does not guarantee.
help.hint_2: Use the layer named in the prompt—working tree, staging area, or history—to test each choice.

options.a: The left column describes staged changes.
options.b: The right column describes working-tree changes.
options.d: Every listed path has already been committed.

fill-blank-choice-1.title: Unstaged Modification Column
fill-blank-choice-1.prompt: In short status, an unstaged tracked-file edit uses the letter `[blank1]` in the right column.
fill-blank-choice-1.hint: Choose the term or symbol that matches the exact state described, not a nearby Git concept.

help.concept: Read full and short status reports to tell untracked files, unstaged edits, staged work, and clean state apart.
help.hint_1: Pay attention to whether the prompt describes an untracked path, a working-tree edit, staged work, or history.
help.hint_2: Read spaces, punctuation, and status columns as meaningful when the question shows Git output.

fill-blank-choice-1.template: An unstaged tracked-file edit uses `[blank1]` in the right column.

fill-blank-choice-2.title: Untracked File Marker
fill-blank-choice-2.prompt: In short status, a file Git has never tracked begins with `[blank1]`.
fill-blank-choice-2.hint: Choose the term or symbol that matches the exact state described, not a nearby Git concept.

help.concept: Read full and short status reports to tell untracked files, unstaged edits, staged work, and clean state apart.
help.hint_1: Pay attention to whether the prompt describes an untracked path, a working-tree edit, staged work, or history.
help.hint_2: Read spaces, punctuation, and status columns as meaningful when the question shows Git output.

fill-blank-choice-2.template: A file Git has never tracked begins with `[blank1]`.

sketch0.title: Read the Full Repository Report
sketch0.bodyMarkdown: `git status` is a read-only report. It names the current branch and groups paths by whether they are staged, unstaged, or untracked. In this two-file project, the first report should show `README.md` and `schedule.txt` as untracked because no snapshot contains them yet.

sketch1.title: Stage the Two-File Proposal
sketch1.bodyMarkdown: `git add README.md schedule.txt` copies the current versions of both files into the staging area—the proposed next snapshot. `git status --short` then uses two columns to summarize state. An `A` in the left column means a new path is staged.

sketch2.title: Read the Staged Summary
sketch2.bodyMarkdown:
After both files are staged, run:

```bash
git diff --staged --stat
```

This command shows a compact summary of **all changes currently in the staging area**—the changes prepared for the next commit.

Its output may look like this:

```text
README.md    | 3 +++
schedule.txt | 3 +++
2 files changed, 6 insertions(+)
```

The lines `README.md | 3 +++` and `schedule.txt | 3 +++` show that each staged file contains three added lines. The final line summarizes the proposed snapshot: two files changed and six total insertions.

This is different from:

```bash
git diff -- schedule.txt
```

That command shows the actual line-by-line **unstaged changes** in `schedule.txt` only. It compares the file in the working tree with its staged version.

Remember:

* `git diff --staged --stat` summarizes everything already staged.
* `git diff -- schedule.txt` displays unstaged line-by-line changes in one file.

Neither command creates a commit or changes any file. They only inspect the repository’s current state.
